content.content: 237df0e Quote is required even if it begins with backquote. (#440)

1→# YAML support for the Go language 2→ 3→[![PkgGoDev](https://pkg.go.dev/badge/github.com/goccy/go-yaml)](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/goccy/go-yaml) 4→![Go](https://github.com/goccy/go-yaml/workflows/Go/badge.svg) 5→[![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/goccy/go-yaml/branch/master/graph/badge.svg)](https://codecov.io/gh/goccy/go-yaml) 6→[![Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/badge/github.com/goccy/go-yaml)](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/goccy/go-yaml) 7→ 8→ 9→ 10→# Why a new library? 11→ 12→As of this writing, there already exists a de facto standard library for YAML processing for Go: [https://github.com/go-yaml/yaml](https://github.com/go-yaml/yaml). However we feel that some features are lacking, namely: 13→ 14→- Pretty format for error notifications 15→- Direct manipulation of YAML abstract syntax tree 16→- Support for `Anchor` and `Alias` when marshaling 17→- Allow referencing elements declared in another file via anchors 18→ 19→# Features 20→ 21→- Pretty format for error notifications 22→- Supports `Scanner` or `Lexer` or `Parser` as public API 23→- Supports `Anchor` and `Alias` to Marshaler 24→- Allow referencing elements declared in another file via anchors 25→- Extract value or AST by YAMLPath ( YAMLPath is like a JSONPath ) 26→ 27→# Installation 28→ 29→```sh 30→go get -u github.com/goccy/go-yaml 31→``` 32→ 33→# Synopsis 34→ 35→## 1. Simple Encode/Decode 36→ 37→Has an interface like `go-yaml/yaml` using `reflect` 38→ 39→```go 40→var v struct { 41→ A int 42→ B string 43→} 44→v.A = 1 45→v.B = "hello" 46→bytes, err := yaml.Marshal(v) 47→if err != nil { 48→ //... 49→} 50→fmt.Println(string(bytes)) // "a: 1\nb: hello\n" 51→``` 52→ 53→```go 54→ yml := ` 55→%YAML 1.2 56→--- 57→a: 1 58→b: c 59→` 60→var v struct { 61→ A int 62→ B string 63→} 64→if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(yml), &v); err != nil { 65→ //... 66→} 67→``` 68→ 69→To control marshal/unmarshal behavior, you can use the `yaml` tag. 70→ 71→```go 72→ yml := `--- 73→foo: 1 74→bar: c 75→` 76→var v struct { 77→ A int `yaml:"foo"` 78→ B string `yaml:"bar"` 79→} 80→if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(yml), &v); err != nil { 81→ //... 82→} 83→``` 84→ 85→For convenience, we also accept the `json` tag. Note that not all options from 86→the `json` tag will have significance when parsing YAML documents. If both 87→tags exist, `yaml` tag will take precedence. 88→ 89→```go 90→ yml := `--- 91→foo: 1 92→bar: c 93→` 94→var v struct { 95→ A int `json:"foo"` 96→ B string `json:"bar"` 97→} 98→if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(yml), &v); err != nil { 99→ //... 100→} 101→``` 102→ 103→For custom marshal/unmarshaling, implement either `Bytes` or `Interface` variant of marshaler/unmarshaler. The difference is that while `BytesMarshaler`/`BytesUnmarshaler` behaves like [`encoding/json`](https://pkg.go.dev/encoding/json) and `InterfaceMarshaler`/`InterfaceUnmarshaler` behaves like [`gopkg.in/yaml.v2`](https://pkg.go.dev/gopkg.in/yaml.v2). 104→ 105→Semantically both are the same, but they differ in performance. Because indentation matters in YAML, you cannot simply accept a valid YAML fragment from a Marshaler, and expect it to work when it is attached to the parent container's serialized form. Therefore when we receive use the `BytesMarshaler`, which returns `[]byte`, we must decode it once to figure out how to make it work in the given context. If you use the `InterfaceMarshaler`, we can skip the decoding. 106→ 107→If you are repeatedly marshaling complex objects, the latter is always better 108→performance wise. But if you are, for example, just providing a choice between 109→a config file format that is read only once, the former is probably easier to 110→code. 111→ 112→## 2. Reference elements declared in another file 113→ 114→`testdata` directory contains `anchor.yml` file: 115→ 116→```shell 117→├── testdata 118→   └── anchor.yml 119→``` 120→ 121→And `anchor.yml` is defined as follows: 122→ 123→```yaml 124→a: &a 125→ b: 1 126→ c: hello 127→``` 128→ 129→Then, if `yaml.ReferenceDirs("testdata")` option is passed to `yaml.Decoder`, 130→ `Decoder` tries to find the anchor definition from YAML files the under `testdata` directory. 131→ 132→```go 133→buf := bytes.NewBufferString("a: *a\n") 134→dec := yaml.NewDecoder(buf, yaml.ReferenceDirs("testdata")) 135→var v struct { 136→ A struct { 137→ B int 138→ C string 139→ } 140→} 141→if err := dec.Decode(&v); err != nil { 142→ //... 143→} 144→fmt.Printf("%+v\n", v) // {A:{B:1 C:hello}} 145→``` 146→ 147→## 3. Encode with `Anchor` and `Alias` 148→ 149→### 3.1. Explicitly declared `Anchor` name and `Alias` name 150→ 151→If you want to use `anchor` or `alias`, you can define it as a struct tag. 152→ 153→```go 154→type T struct { 155→ A int 156→ B string 157→} 158→var v struct { 159→ C *T `yaml:"c,anchor=x"` 160→ D *T `yaml:"d,alias=x"` 161→} 162→v.C = &T{A: 1, B: "hello"} 163→v.D = v.C 164→bytes, err := yaml.Marshal(v) 165→if err != nil { 166→ panic(err) 167→} 168→fmt.Println(string(bytes)) 169→/* 170→c: &x 171→ a: 1 172→ b: hello 173→d: *x 174→*/ 175→``` 176→ 177→### 3.2. Implicitly declared `Anchor` and `Alias` names 178→ 179→If you do not explicitly declare the anchor name, the default behavior is to 180→use the equivalent of `strings.ToLower($FieldName)` as the name of the anchor. 181→ 182→If you do not explicitly declare the alias name AND the value is a pointer 183→to another element, we look up the anchor name by finding out which anchor 184→field the value is assigned to by looking up its pointer address. 185→ 186→```go 187→type T struct { 188→ I int 189→ S string 190→} 191→var v struct { 192→ A *T `yaml:"a,anchor"` 193→ B *T `yaml:"b,anchor"` 194→ C *T `yaml:"c,alias"` 195→ D *T `yaml:"d,alias"` 196→} 197→v.A = &T{I: 1, S: "hello"} 198→v.B = &T{I: 2, S: "world"} 199→v.C = v.A // C has same pointer address to A 200→v.D = v.B // D has same pointer address to B 201→bytes, err := yaml.Marshal(v) 202→if err != nil { 203→ //... 204→} 205→fmt.Println(string(bytes)) 206→/* 207→a: &a 208→ i: 1 209→ s: hello 210→b: &b 211→ i: 2 212→ s: world 213→c: *a 214→d: *b 215→*/ 216→``` 217→ 218→### 3.3 MergeKey and Alias 219→ 220→Merge key and alias ( `<<: *alias` ) can be used by embedding a structure with the `inline,alias` tag. 221→ 222→```go 223→type Person struct { 224→ *Person `yaml:",omitempty,inline,alias"` // embed Person type for default value 225→ Name string `yaml:",omitempty"` 226→ Age int `yaml:",omitempty"` 227→} 228→defaultPerson := &Person{ 229→ Name: "John Smith", 230→ Age: 20, 231→} 232→people := []*Person{ 233→ { 234→ Person: defaultPerson, // assign default value 235→ Name: "Ken", // override Name property 236→ Age: 10, // override Age property 237→ }, 238→ { 239→ Person: defaultPerson, // assign default value only 240→ }, 241→} 242→var doc struct { 243→ Default *Person `yaml:"default,anchor"` 244→ People []*Person `yaml:"people"` 245→} 246→doc.Default = defaultPerson 247→doc.People = people 248→bytes, err := yaml.Marshal(doc) 249→if err != nil { 250→ //... 251→} 252→fmt.Println(string(bytes)) 253→/* 254→default: &default 255→ name: John Smith 256→ age: 20 257→people: 258→- <<: *default 259→ name: Ken 260→ age: 10 261→- <<: *default 262→*/ 263→``` 264→ 265→## 4. Pretty Formatted Errors 266→ 267→Error values produced during parsing have two extra features over regular 268→error values. 269→ 270→First, by default, they contain extra information on the location of the error 271→from the source YAML document, to make it easier to find the error location. 272→ 273→Second, the error messages can optionally be colorized. 274→ 275→If you would like to control exactly how the output looks like, consider 276→using `yaml.FormatError`, which accepts two boolean values to 277→control turning these features on or off. 278→ 279→ 280→ 281→## 5. Use YAMLPath 282→ 283→```go 284→yml := ` 285→store: 286→ book: 287→ - author: john 288→ price: 10 289→ - author: ken 290→ price: 12 291→ bicycle: 292→ color: red 293→ price: 19.95 294→` 295→path, err := yaml.PathString("$.store.book[*].author") 296→if err != nil { 297→ //... 298→} 299→var authors []string 300→if err := path.Read(strings.NewReader(yml), &authors); err != nil { 301→ //... 302→} 303→fmt.Println(authors) 304→// [john ken] 305→``` 306→ 307→### 5.1 Print customized error with YAML source code 308→ 309→```go 310→package main 311→ 312→import ( 313→ "fmt" 314→ 315→ "github.com/goccy/go-yaml" 316→) 317→ 318→func main() { 319→ yml := ` 320→a: 1 321→b: "hello" 322→` 323→ var v struct { 324→ A int 325→ B string 326→ } 327→ if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(yml), &v); err != nil { 328→ panic(err) 329→ } 330→ if v.A != 2 { 331→ // output error with YAML source 332→ path, err := yaml.PathString("$.a") 333→ if err != nil { 334→ panic(err) 335→ } 336→ source, err := path.AnnotateSource([]byte(yml), true) 337→ if err != nil { 338→ panic(err) 339→ } 340→ fmt.Printf("a value expected 2 but actual %d:\n%s\n", v.A, string(source)) 341→ } 342→} 343→``` 344→ 345→output result is the following: 346→ 347→ 348→ 349→ 350→# Tools 351→ 352→## ycat 353→ 354→print yaml file with color 355→ 356→ 357→ 358→### Installation 359→ 360→```sh 361→go install github.com/goccy/go-yaml/cmd/ycat@latest 362→``` 363→ 364→# Looking for Sponsors 365→ 366→I'm looking for sponsors this library. This library is being developed as a personal project in my spare time. If you want a quick response or problem resolution when using this library in your project, please register as a [sponsor](https://github.com/sponsors/goccy). I will cooperate as much as possible. Of course, this library is developed as an MIT license, so you can use it freely for free. 367→ 368→# License 369→ 370→MIT 371→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

content.text:
Now I have enough information. Let me provide a comprehensive report:

## Comprehensive YAML Decoder Analysis Report

### 1. Repository Overview

This is **go-yaml** (`github.com/goccy/go-yaml`), a complete YAML parser and encoder written in pure Go. It provides:
- Full YAML 1.2 support with custom AST manipulation
- Reflection-based marshaling/unmarshaling like the standard library
- Advanced features: anchors, aliases, merge keys, external file references
- Pretty-formatted error reporting with source location

**Repository Structure:**
- `/decode.go` - Main decoder logic (1797 lines)
- `/encode.go` - Main encoder logic
- `/struct.go` - Struct field metadata and tag parsing
- `/parser/` - YAML token parsing to AST
- `/ast/` - Abstract Syntax Tree node definitions
- `/lexer/`, `/scanner/`, `/token/` - Lexical analysis pipeline
- `/testdata/` - Test fixtures

---

### 2. Struct Decoding Architecture

#### 2.1 Struct Field Metadata (`struct.go`)

The decoder processes struct tags to extract rendering and encoding information:

```go
type StructField struct {
    FieldName    string  // Go field name
    RenderName   string  // YAML key name (default: lowercase FieldName)
    AnchorName   string  // YAML anchor name
    AliasName    string  // YAML alias reference
    IsAutoAnchor bool    // Auto-generate anchor name
    IsAutoAlias  bool    // Auto-generate alias reference
    IsOmitEmpty  bool    // Skip encoding if zero value
    IsFlow       bool    // Use flow style (inline {})
    IsInline     bool    // Embed struct fields directly
}
```

Supported tags: `yaml:"name,option1,option2"` or `json:"name,option1,option2"` (yaml takes precedence).

Tag options:
- `omitempty` - Omit from encoding if zero value
- `flow` - Use flow style
- `inline` - Embed fields into parent (for embedded structs)
- `anchor[=name]` - Create YAML anchor (with optional explicit name)
- `alias[=name]` - Create YAML alias (with optional explicit name)

#### 2.2 Main Struct Decoding Function (`Decoder.decodeStruct`)

**Location:** `decode.go:1181-1347`

**Key Flow:**

1. **Early Exit on Null:** If source node is nil, returns immediately
2. **Field Map Creation:** Builds map of struct fields from reflection, extracting metadata
3. **Key-to-Node Mapping:** Creates mapping of YAML keys to AST nodes via `keyToValueNodeMap`
4. **Two Decoding Paths:**

   **Path A: Inline Fields** (lines 1223-1276)
   - For embedded structs with `inline` tag
   - Converts keyToNodeMap into a synthetic MappingNode
   - Special handling for pointer inline fields when source is NullType
   - Calls `setDefaultValueIfConflicted` to zero-initialize conflicting fields

   **Path B: Regular Fields** (lines 1278-1304)
   - For normal struct fields
   - Looks up field's RenderName in keyToNodeMap
   - Skips if field not present in YAML

5. **Value Decoding:** For each field found:
   - Calls `createDecodedNewValue(ctx, fieldType, currentValue, node)`
   - Sets the decoded value via reflection
   - Casts to assignable type (handles pointer wrapping)

---

### 3. Null/Empty Node Handling

#### 3.1 Critical Null Handling Points

**Point 1: Parser Creates Null Nodes** (`parser/parser.go:152-215`)

When a mapping has a key but no value, the parser automatically creates a `NullNode`:
```yaml
key:           # <- null value created here
next_key: val
```

Multiple scenarios trigger null creation:
- End of input after colon
- Column position mismatch (indicates new key)
- Explicit empty value

**Point 2: `createDecodedNewValue` Returns Zero Value** (lines 982-1006)

```go
func (d *Decoder) createDecodedNewValue(
    ctx context.Context, typ reflect.Type, defaultVal reflect.Value, node ast.Node,
) (reflect.Value, error) {
    // ... Handle aliases ...
    
    // CRITICAL: Null nodes result in zero value
    if node.Type() == ast.NullType {
        return reflect.Zero(typ), nil  // Returns zero-initialized value
    }
    
    newValue := d.createDecodableValue(typ)
    // ...
}
```

This means:
- Null YAML nodes → Go zero values
- For pointers: `nil`
- For structs: all fields zero-initialized
- For slices: empty slice

**Point 3: Pointer Null Handling** (lines 1237-1240, 1284-1287)

Special checks before decoding pointer fields when source is NullType:
```go
if fieldValue.Type().Kind() == reflect.Ptr && src.Type() == ast.NullType {
    // set nil value to pointer
    fieldValue.Set(reflect.Zero(fieldValue.Type()))
    continue
}
```

This applies to BOTH inline and regular fields when the **entire source struct** is null.

**Point 4: `getMapNode` Returns nil for Null** (lines 418-445)

```go
func (d *Decoder) getMapNode(node ast.Node) (ast.MapNode, error) {
    if _, ok := node.(*ast.NullNode); ok {
        return nil, nil  // Returns nil, not error
    }
    // ...
}
```

This allows graceful handling of null values in maps and arrays:
```go
if mapNode == nil {
    return nil  // Early exit, no decoding
}
```

#### 3.2 Empty YAML Nodes Handling

Empty mappings `{}` and empty sequences `[]` are parsed as valid (non-null) nodes with zero elements.

**For Maps:** `decodeMap` (lines 1507-1579)
- mapNode is not nil
- MapRange returns empty iterator
- Result: empty map created

**For Structs within Empty Mapping:**
```yaml
outer:        # This value becomes {}
  inner: val
```
Empty mappings have no keys, so `keyToNodeMap` is empty → all fields stay zero-initialized.

---

### 4. Nested Struct Decoding Behavior

#### 4.1 Non-Inline Nested Structs

```yaml
outer:
  inner: value
```

When decoding a nested struct:
1. Parser creates MappingNode for `outer`'s value
2. `decodeStruct` is called recursively for the nested struct type
3. New keyToNodeMap built from nested MappingNode
4. Fields decoded from nested scope

#### 4.2 Inline Struct Decoding (Critical Path)

```go
if structField.IsInline {
    // Line 1242-1246: Create synthetic mapping from ALL parent's keys
    mapNode := ast.Mapping(nil, false)
    for k, v := range keyToNodeMap {  // Parent's keyToNodeMap
        key := &ast.StringNode{...}
        mapNode.Values = append(mapNode.Values, ast.MappingValue(nil, key, v))
    }
    
    // Line 1247: Decode inline struct with synthetic mapping
    newFieldValue, err := d.createDecodedNewValue(ctx, fieldValue.Type(), fieldValue, mapNode)
}
```

**Key Insight:** Inline structs receive **all parent-level keys**, not just their named fields. The inline struct's field names must match parent keys.

#### 4.3 Conflict Resolution (`setDefaultValueIfConflicted`)

When an inline struct's field names conflict with parent struct field names:
```go
// If parent has "name" and inline child has "name"
if !fieldMap.isIncludedRenderName(structField.RenderName) {
    continue
}
// if declared same key name, set default value
fieldValue.Set(reflect.Zero(fieldValue.Type()))
```

Conflicting embedded fields are **zero-initialized** even if they had values.

---

### 5. Zero-Initialization Patterns

The decoder enforces zero-initialization in these scenarios:

| Scenario | Line(s) | Behavior |
|----------|---------|----------|
| Null YAML value | 992-993 | `reflect.Zero(typ)` returned |
| Pointer field + NullType source | 1237-1240, 1284-1287 | Set to nil |
| Inline field + NullType source | 1237-1240 | Set to nil |
| Missing field in YAML | (implicit) | Already zero from reflection |
| Empty mapping value | (implicit) | Zero-init all nested fields |
| Conflicting inline field | 1086 | `reflect.Zero(fieldValue.Type())` |
| Array null element | 1366-1368 | `reflect.Zero(elemType)` for pointers |
| Slice null element | 1405-1407 | `reflect.Zero(elemType)` for pointers |
| Map null value | 1556-1559 | `reflect.Zero(valueType)` for pointers |

---

### 6. nodeToValue Conversion

**Location:** `decode.go:263-353`

The `nodeToValue` function converts AST nodes to Go interface{} values:
- NullNode → `nil`
- StringNode → string
- IntegerNode → int64 or uint64
- BoolNode → bool
- MappingNode → `map[string]interface{}`
- SequenceNode → `[]interface{}`
- AnchorNode → recursively converts wrapped value
- AliasNode → looks up anchor in anchorNodeMap

**Important:** Returns nil for NullNode, allowing type assertions to fail gracefully.

---

### 7. Test Coverage for Null/Empty

Key test cases in `decode_test.go`:

```go
// Null values in maps
"v: null" → map[string]interface{}{"v": nil}

// Empty mappings
"a:" → map[string]interface{}{"a": nil}

// Empty with multiple keys
"a:\nb:\nc:" → {"a": nil, "b": nil, "c": nil}

// Nested null
"a:\n  b:" → {"a": map[string]interface{}{"b": nil}}

// Inline structs
"a: 1\nb: 2\nChild `yaml:\",inline\"`" → Works with merged fields
```

---

### 8. Critical Code Locations Reference

| Concept | File | Lines | Function |
|---------|------|-------|----------|
| Struct field metadata | struct.go | 39-80 | `structField()` |
| Main struct decoding | decode.go | 1181-1347 | `Decoder.decodeStruct()` |
| Null value handling | decode.go | 992-993 | `createDecodedNewValue()` |
| Pointer null handling | decode.go | 1237-1240, 1284-1287 | `decodeStruct()` |
| Map/Array null checks | decode.go | 418-477 | `getMapNode()`, `getArrayNode()` |
| Inline conflict resolution | decode.go | 1065-1090 | `setDefaultValueIfConflicted()` |
| AST to value conversion | decode.go | 263-353 | `nodeToValue()` |
| Null node creation | parser/parser.go | 152-215 | `createMapValueNode()` |

---

### 9. Summary

**The YAML decoder handles null and empty nodes through a carefully orchestrated system:**

1. **Parser Phase:** Automatically creates NullNode AST nodes for missing values
2. **Mapping Phase:** `keyToValueNodeMap` maps keys to their value nodes
3. **Decoding Phase:** `createDecodedNewValue` detects NullType and returns `reflect.Zero()`
4. **Assignment Phase:** Values assigned via reflection, with special pointer handling
5. **Conflict Resolution:** Inline struct conflicts resolved by zero-initialization

The pattern is consistent: **null/missing YAML values always result in Go zero values**, with special logic for pointer types (which receive `nil` instead of pointer-to-zero).

content.text:
Now I have enough information. Let me provide a comprehensive report:

## Comprehensive YAML Decoder Analysis Report

### 1. Repository Overview

This is **go-yaml** (`github.com/goccy/go-yaml`), a complete YAML parser and encoder written in pure Go. It provides:
- Full YAML 1.2 support with custom AST manipulation
- Reflection-based marshaling/unmarshaling like the standard library
- Advanced features: anchors, aliases, merge keys, external file references
- Pretty-formatted error reporting with source location

**Repository Structure:**
- `/decode.go` - Main decoder logic (1797 lines)
- `/encode.go` - Main encoder logic
- `/struct.go` - Struct field metadata and tag parsing
- `/parser/` - YAML token parsing to AST
- `/ast/` - Abstract Syntax Tree node definitions
- `/lexer/`, `/scanner/`, `/token/` - Lexical analysis pipeline
- `/testdata/` - Test fixtures

---

### 2. Struct Decoding Architecture

#### 2.1 Struct Field Metadata (`struct.go`)

The decoder processes struct tags to extract rendering and encoding information:

```go
type StructField struct {
    FieldName    string  // Go field name
    RenderName   string  // YAML key name (default: lowercase FieldName)
    AnchorName   string  // YAML anchor name
    AliasName    string  // YAML alias reference
    IsAutoAnchor bool    // Auto-generate anchor name
    IsAutoAlias  bool    // Auto-generate alias reference
    IsOmitEmpty  bool    // Skip encoding if zero value
    IsFlow       bool    // Use flow style (inline {})
    IsInline     bool    // Embed struct fields directly
}
```

Supported tags: `yaml:"name,option1,option2"` or `json:"name,option1,option2"` (yaml takes precedence).

Tag options:
- `omitempty` - Omit from encoding if zero value
- `flow` - Use flow style
- `inline` - Embed fields into parent (for embedded structs)
- `anchor[=name]` - Create YAML anchor (with optional explicit name)
- `alias[=name]` - Create YAML alias (with optional explicit name)

#### 2.2 Main Struct Decoding Function (`Decoder.decodeStruct`)

**Location:** `decode.go:1181-1347`

**Key Flow:**

1. **Early Exit on Null:** If source node is nil, returns immediately
2. **Field Map Creation:** Builds map of struct fields from reflection, extracting metadata
3. **Key-to-Node Mapping:** Creates mapping of YAML keys to AST nodes via `keyToValueNodeMap`
4. **Two Decoding Paths:**

   **Path A: Inline Fields** (lines 1223-1276)
   - For embedded structs with `inline` tag
   - Converts keyToNodeMap into a synthetic MappingNode
   - Special handling for pointer inline fields when source is NullType
   - Calls `setDefaultValueIfConflicted` to zero-initialize conflicting fields

   **Path B: Regular Fields** (lines 1278-1304)
   - For normal struct fields
   - Looks up field's RenderName in keyToNodeMap
   - Skips if field not present in YAML

5. **Value Decoding:** For each field found:
   - Calls `createDecodedNewValue(ctx, fieldType, currentValue, node)`
   - Sets the decoded value via reflection
   - Casts to assignable type (handles pointer wrapping)

---

### 3. Null/Empty Node Handling

#### 3.1 Critical Null Handling Points

**Point 1: Parser Creates Null Nodes** (`parser/parser.go:152-215`)

When a mapping has a key but no value, the parser automatically creates a `NullNode`:
```yaml
key:           # <- null value created here
next_key: val
```

Multiple scenarios trigger null creation:
- End of input after colon
- Column position mismatch (indicates new key)
- Explicit empty value

**Point 2: `createDecodedNewValue` Returns Zero Value** (lines 982-1006)

```go
func (d *Decoder) createDecodedNewValue(
    ctx context.Context, typ reflect.Type, defaultVal reflect.Value, node ast.Node,
) (reflect.Value, error) {
    // ... Handle aliases ...
    
    // CRITICAL: Null nodes result in zero value
    if node.Type() == ast.NullType {
        return reflect.Zero(typ), nil  // Returns zero-initialized value
    }
    
    newValue := d.createDecodableValue(typ)
    // ...
}
```

This means:
- Null YAML nodes → Go zero values
- For pointers: `nil`
- For structs: all fields zero-initialized
- For slices: empty slice

**Point 3: Pointer Null Handling** (lines 1237-1240, 1284-1287)

Special checks before decoding pointer fields when source is NullType:
```go
if fieldValue.Type().Kind() == reflect.Ptr && src.Type() == ast.NullType {
    // set nil value to pointer
    fieldValue.Set(reflect.Zero(fieldValue.Type()))
    continue
}
```

This applies to BOTH inline and regular fields when the **entire source struct** is null.

**Point 4: `getMapNode` Returns nil for Null** (lines 418-445)

```go
func (d *Decoder) getMapNode(node ast.Node) (ast.MapNode, error) {
    if _, ok := node.(*ast.NullNode); ok {
        return nil, nil  // Returns nil, not error
    }
    // ...
}
```

This allows graceful handling of null values in maps and arrays:
```go
if mapNode == nil {
    return nil  // Early exit, no decoding
}
```

#### 3.2 Empty YAML Nodes Handling

Empty mappings `{}` and empty sequences `[]` are parsed as valid (non-null) nodes with zero elements.

**For Maps:** `decodeMap` (lines 1507-1579)
- mapNode is not nil
- MapRange returns empty iterator
- Result: empty map created

**For Structs within Empty Mapping:**
```yaml
outer:        # This value becomes {}
  inner: val
```
Empty mappings have no keys, so `keyToNodeMap` is empty → all fields stay zero-initialized.

---

### 4. Nested Struct Decoding Behavior

#### 4.1 Non-Inline Nested Structs

```yaml
outer:
  inner: value
```

When decoding a nested struct:
1. Parser creates MappingNode for `outer`'s value
2. `decodeStruct` is called recursively for the nested struct type
3. New keyToNodeMap built from nested MappingNode
4. Fields decoded from nested scope

#### 4.2 Inline Struct Decoding (Critical Path)

```go
if structField.IsInline {
    // Line 1242-1246: Create synthetic mapping from ALL parent's keys
    mapNode := ast.Mapping(nil, false)
    for k, v := range keyToNodeMap {  // Parent's keyToNodeMap
        key := &ast.StringNode{...}
        mapNode.Values = append(mapNode.Values, ast.MappingValue(nil, key, v))
    }
    
    // Line 1247: Decode inline struct with synthetic mapping
    newFieldValue, err := d.createDecodedNewValue(ctx, fieldValue.Type(), fieldValue, mapNode)
}
```

**Key Insight:** Inline structs receive **all parent-level keys**, not just their named fields. The inline struct's field names must match parent keys.

#### 4.3 Conflict Resolution (`setDefaultValueIfConflicted`)

When an inline struct's field names conflict with parent struct field names:
```go
// If parent has "name" and inline child has "name"
if !fieldMap.isIncludedRenderName(structField.RenderName) {
    continue
}
// if declared same key name, set default value
fieldValue.Set(reflect.Zero(fieldValue.Type()))
```

Conflicting embedded fields are **zero-initialized** even if they had values.

---

### 5. Zero-Initialization Patterns

The decoder enforces zero-initialization in these scenarios:

| Scenario | Line(s) | Behavior |
|----------|---------|----------|
| Null YAML value | 992-993 | `reflect.Zero(typ)` returned |
| Pointer field + NullType source | 1237-1240, 1284-1287 | Set to nil |
| Inline field + NullType source | 1237-1240 | Set to nil |
| Missing field in YAML | (implicit) | Already zero from reflection |
| Empty mapping value | (implicit) | Zero-init all nested fields |
| Conflicting inline field | 1086 | `reflect.Zero(fieldValue.Type())` |
| Array null element | 1366-1368 | `reflect.Zero(elemType)` for pointers |
| Slice null element | 1405-1407 | `reflect.Zero(elemType)` for pointers |
| Map null value | 1556-1559 | `reflect.Zero(valueType)` for pointers |

---

### 6. nodeToValue Conversion

**Location:** `decode.go:263-353`

The `nodeToValue` function converts AST nodes to Go interface{} values:
- NullNode → `nil`
- StringNode → string
- IntegerNode → int64 or uint64
- BoolNode → bool
- MappingNode → `map[string]interface{}`
- SequenceNode → `[]interface{}`
- AnchorNode → recursively converts wrapped value
- AliasNode → looks up anchor in anchorNodeMap

**Important:** Returns nil for NullNode, allowing type assertions to fail gracefully.

---

### 7. Test Coverage for Null/Empty

Key test cases in `decode_test.go`:

```go
// Null values in maps
"v: null" → map[string]interface{}{"v": nil}

// Empty mappings
"a:" → map[string]interface{}{"a": nil}

// Empty with multiple keys
"a:\nb:\nc:" → {"a": nil, "b": nil, "c": nil}

// Nested null
"a:\n  b:" → {"a": map[string]interface{}{"b": nil}}

// Inline structs
"a: 1\nb: 2\nChild `yaml:\",inline\"`" → Works with merged fields
```

---

### 8. Critical Code Locations Reference

| Concept | File | Lines | Function |
|---------|------|-------|----------|
| Struct field metadata | struct.go | 39-80 | `structField()` |
| Main struct decoding | decode.go | 1181-1347 | `Decoder.decodeStruct()` |
| Null value handling | decode.go | 992-993 | `createDecodedNewValue()` |
| Pointer null handling | decode.go | 1237-1240, 1284-1287 | `decodeStruct()` |
| Map/Array null checks | decode.go | 418-477 | `getMapNode()`, `getArrayNode()` |
| Inline conflict resolution | decode.go | 1065-1090 | `setDefaultValueIfConflicted()` |
| AST to value conversion | decode.go | 263-353 | `nodeToValue()` |
| Null node creation | parser/parser.go | 152-215 | `createMapValueNode()` |

---

### 9. Summary

**The YAML decoder handles null and empty nodes through a carefully orchestrated system:**

1. **Parser Phase:** Automatically creates NullNode AST nodes for missing values
2. **Mapping Phase:** `keyToValueNodeMap` maps keys to their value nodes
3. **Decoding Phase:** `createDecodedNewValue` detects NullType and returns `reflect.Zero()`
4. **Assignment Phase:** Values assigned via reflection, with special pointer handling
5. **Conflict Resolution:** Inline struct conflicts resolved by zero-initialization

The pattern is consistent: **null/missing YAML values always result in Go zero values**, with special logic for pointer types (which receive `nil` instead of pointer-to-zero).

Now I have a good understanding of the codebase. Let me look at the specific issue:

The decoder overrides the default values of a nested struct with zero-initialised values when the YAML node is null or contains only comments.

The issue is in `createDecodedNewValue` at line 992-993:
```go
if node.Type() == ast.NullType {
    return reflect.Zero(typ), nil  // Returns zero-initialized value
}
```

This returns a zero value for null nodes, which then gets assigned to the struct field, overriding any default values.

The fix should be: when the node is null AND the `defaultVal` is not the zero value, we should return the `defaultVal` instead of `reflect.Zero(typ)`.


But I need to be careful with pointer fields—if a pointer field is null in YAML, it should remain nil rather than being replaced with a default. Let me trace through the actual `createDecodedNewValue` function to see how `defaultVal` is handled and understand the nested struct decoding flow, particularly when the YAML has a null node for a field like `second:` with only comments.

The core issue is that when we encounter a null node for a struct type, we're returning a zero-initialized struct that overwrites any default value that was set. The fix should preserve the default value in this case—only zero out the struct if there's no meaningful default to fall back on.

Now I understand the bug. In `createDecodedNewValue` (line 992-993), when a null node is encountered, it returns `reflect.Zero(typ)` which ignores the `defaultVal` parameter — even though `defaultVal` holds the existing struct field value with pre-set defaults.

The fix: for struct types (which can't be nil in Go), return the `defaultVal` instead of zero when the node is null.

The fix works. Here's a summary:

**Root cause:** In `createDecodedNewValue` (`decode.go:992-993`), when the YAML node is null, the function returned `reflect.Zero(typ)` — ignoring the `defaultVal` parameter entirely. This zeroed out any pre-set default values in nested structs.

**Fix:** For `reflect.Struct` kinds, return `defaultVal` instead of zero when the node is null. Structs in Go can't be nil, so a null YAML node should mean "no values provided", not "zero everything out". Pointer types continue to get the zero (nil) treatment unchanged.
